title: SIAVASHHABIBI.io
text:
Glass Panel Load Calculation


Interactive calculator for glass panel load capacity and thickness design per structural glazing standards.



Mohr’s Circle


Interactive Mohr’s Circle for stress transformation and principal stress visualization.



Flexural Resistance of a Reinforced Concrete Beam


Interactive calculator for the flexural resistance of reinforced concrete beams per CSA design standards.



Section Properties Calculator


Interactive calculator for cross-section properties including moment of inertia, centroid, and section modulus.



Analysis of a Simply Supported Beam


Interactive analysis of a simply supported beam with shear force and bending moment diagrams.



Soil Pressure Coefficients


Interactive visualization of active and passive soil pressure coefficients using Rankine and Coulomb theories.



Structural Wood Panels


Interactive reference and design tool for structural wood panels and sheathing properties.

title: ETABSPY
text:
A Python desktop application that automates the shear wall pier force extraction and S-CONCRETE file generation workflow for multi-story building structural analysis. Built to replace a tedious manual process — retrieving pier forces from ETABS, copying results into Excel, post-processing spreadsheets, and manually preparing S-CONCRETE input files — with a single, controlled, automated pipeline.



ETABSPY V2.0


ETABSPY connects directly to an analyzed ETABS model via COM API, extracts all required datasets (geometry, load cases, load combinations, pier forces, material properties), and generates ready-to-use .sco S-CONCRETE design files in seconds. Once data is retrieved, it can be saved as a .pkl snapshot and reloaded later without needing ETABS open — enabling a fully offline processing mode.



ETABS


Key engineering logic includes seismic shear force magnification (Rd application), where ETABSPY deconstructs each load combination into its individual load cases, applies the user-defined seismic magnification factor only to seismic shear components (V2, V3), then reconstructs the combination to produce the final magnified forces. It also computes moment distribution factors (Cm) and organizes output files by story grouping, with user control over how stories are batched into each S-CONCRETE file via the Pier Forces Viewer.






Live ETABS Integration — Connects to running ETABS instances via COM API to extract model data in real time
SCONCRETE File Generation — Automatically generates .sco analysis files from pier forces and load combinations
Seismic Load Magnification — Implements vectorized seismic magnification algorithms across thousands of load combinations
Pier Forces Viewer — Interactive step chart visualization of pier forces across building stories
Data Persistence — Save and load model snapshots via .pkl files for offline processing
High-Performance Data Processing — Handles millions of rows using optimized pandas DataFrame operations
Responsive GUI — PyQt5 interface with background threading to keep UI non-blocking during heavy operations
Centralized Logging — SQLite-based analytics and session logging for corporate deployment tracking
Network Security — Domain validation and connectivity checks for enterprise environments








Layer
Technologies




GUI
PyQt5


Data Processing
pandas, NumPy


ETABS Integration
comtypes, pywin32 (COM API)


Persistence
pickle, SQLite


Packaging
PyInstaller, setup.py

title: About
text:
My programming journey began at the University of Toronto, where I worked as a teaching assistant and MASc student, developing a subroutine for Vector2—a non-linear finite element software (vectoranalysisgroup.com). This experience ignited my passion for combining structural engineering with software development.
That passion evolved into full-stack web development when I created a resource management SaaS for my former company using Laravel and Vue.js. Currently, I’m developing ETABSPY and SAFEPY—Python programs that connect to ETABS and SAFE to retrieve structural analysis data and automate design tasks such as shear wall design and slab design, leveraging data science libraries like NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib for analysis and visualization. EngSheet continues this journey, built as a single-page application with Vue.js that runs blazingly fast and works seamlessly in the browser, with interactive visualizations powered by D3.js.

title: About
text:
My Passion for Running
Running has become a big part of who I am. What started as just a way to stay active slowly turned into something I actually look forward to—a space to think, reset, and challenge myself. There’s something about the rhythm of running that helps me find focus; it clears my head and reminds me that progress usually comes one step at a time. I’m not chasing records—I’m chasing consistency. Whether I’m training for a 5K or just getting out for a quick evening run, it’s where I test my limits, process my thoughts, and find a bit of balance in everything else going on.
You can follow my runs and training journey on Strava.

title: About
section: Staying Active: Boxing, Tennis, and the Gym
text:
Staying Active: Boxing, Tennis, and the Gym
Beyond running, I stay active through boxing, tennis, and regular gym sessions. Boxing teaches me discipline and focus—every round demands presence and precision. Tennis brings out my competitive side while keeping things fun and social. The gym ties it all together, building the strength and endurance that support everything else. These activities aren’t just about fitness; they’re about maintaining balance, pushing boundaries, and finding different ways to challenge myself physically and mentally.

title: About
text:
My Passion for Piano
Playing piano has always been a personal escape for me, a way to slow down and express thoughts that words can’t quite capture. I’m drawn to how a few keys and chords can shift a whole mood, and how much focus and patience it takes to bring a piece to life. It’s where I learn balance between precision and creativity, discipline and emotion. Whether I’m learning a new song or just improvising, piano is my reset button—something that keeps me grounded while letting my mind wander freely.

title: ETABS AI Agent
text:
Control ETABS with plain English.
Describe what you want to do — draw elements, assign properties, retrieve results, switch units — and the agent handles the rest. No API documentation. No parameter mapping. No boilerplate.

“Connect to ETABS, draw a 24×24 area element at the origin, and assign the Default property to it.”







Agent conversation UI






The ETABS AI Agent is a conversational interface that translates natural language instructions into live ETABS COM API calls. It connects directly to a running ETABS instance on your machine and executes operations in real time.
You can:

Create and modify area, frame, and point objects
Assign section properties, load patterns, and boundary conditions
Query model data — pier forces, joint coordinates, section assignments
Switch units, check model state, chain multi-step workflows
Follow up, correct, and iterate — all within the same session



The ETABS connection is established once and persists for the entire conversation. Every subsequent operation runs against the same live model without reconnecting — no state is lost between turns.



The agent maintains full conversation history throughout the session. This means you can ask follow-up questions that reference previous results, drill into something the agent just returned, or build on earlier operations naturally — exactly as you would in a conversation with a colleague.

“How many of those pier labels have forces above 100 kips?” “Now switch to kip-ft and re-run that query.” “Assign the same property to all the elements you just created.”

Each response is informed by everything that came before it in the session.





The agent uses Claude to interpret your intent and select the appropriate ETABS API method. A two-stage routing system keeps token usage efficient: Claude first identifies the relevant API category, then executes the specific method — giving it access to the full ETABS API surface without overloading a single call.
Operations are dispatched directly to the ETABS COM interface and results are returned immediately. Changes appear live in the open model.




User Prompt
    │
    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  Stage 1 — Intent Routing   │
│  LLM classifies task scope  │
└──────────────┬──────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  Stage 2 — Execution        │
│  LLM selects & calls tools  │
└──────────────┬──────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  COM Dispatcher             │
│  Routes to ETABS API        │
└──────────────┬──────────────┘
               │
               ▼
          ETABS Model




Connect to ETABS and draw a new area element with corners at
(0,0,0), (24,0,0), (24,24,0), (0,24,0). Name it TestWall.
Connect to ETABS, check the current units, switch to kip-ft if
they aren't already, then confirm the change.
Connect to ETABS, retrieve the pier forces table, and tell me
how many unique pier labels are in the model.





Anthropic Claude API — natural language understanding and tool use
ETABSPY — core ETABS connection utilities
comtypes / pywin32 — Windows COM interface to ETABS





ETABSPY — the companion desktop application for automated pier force extraction and S-CONCRETE file generation, built on the same core utilities.




MIT
